Home / Burn Rate Calculator

Burn Rate Calculator

Calculate your net monthly burn rate by comparing starting vs ending cash over a period. See how different cash levels affect your runway.

How to Use This Tool

1
Enter your starting cash balance at the beginning of the period.
2
Enter your ending cash balance at the end of the period.
3
Enter the number of months in the period.
4
Review your net burn rate per month and annualized burn rate.
5
Check your estimated runway based on your current cash and burn rate.
6
Scroll down to see 5 comparison scenarios with different starting cash levels.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between net burn and gross burn?

Gross burn is your total monthly operating expenses before any revenue. Net burn is gross burn minus monthly revenue. For example, if you spend $20K/month and earn $5K/month, your gross burn is $20K and net burn is $15K. Always track both — gross burn shows your spending discipline, net burn shows how fast your bank account shrinks.

What is a healthy burn rate for a SaaS startup?

For early-stage SaaS, a net burn of $10K-$50K/month is common post-seed. The key metric is burn multiple: net burn divided by net new ARR per month. A burn multiple under 1.0x is excellent, meaning every dollar of burn generates more than a dollar of new ARR.

How do I reduce my burn rate quickly?

Start with the biggest line items: people (consider contractors instead of full-time hires), office space (go remote), SaaS subscriptions (audit and cancel unused tools), and marketing (shift to organic channels like SEO and content). A 20% reduction in burn can double your runway.

Should I include one-time expenses in burn rate?

No. Burn rate should reflect recurring operating expenses, not one-time costs like equipment purchases or legal fees for incorporation. Track one-time costs separately. This gives you a clearer picture of your ongoing cash needs.

How often should I recalculate my burn rate?

Monthly, at minimum. Review your burn rate every time you close your books. Set up a simple spreadsheet or use accounting software to track it. If your burn rate is increasing month-over-month, investigate immediately — it is easy to let spending creep up without noticing.

Results

Copy All
#1 🔥 Burn Rate Analysis • Starting Cash: $100,000 • Ending Cash: $70,000 • Period: 6 months • Total Cash Spent: $30,000 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ • Net Burn Rate: $5,000/month • Annualized Burn: $60,000/year • Estimated Runway: 20.0 months • Monthly Cash Decline: 5.0% 💡 Tip: Track both net burn (total cash outflow minus revenue) and gross burn (total cash outflow only). Net burn tells you how fast your bank account shrinks. Gross burn tells you your total spending before revenue kicks in.
#2 Starting with $10,000: Burn $1,667/mo → 2.0 months of runway
#3 Starting with $25,000: Burn $4,167/mo → 5.0 months of runway
#4 Starting with $75,000: Burn $12,500/mo → 15.0 months of runway
#5 Starting with $100,000: Burn $16,667/mo → 20.0 months of runway
